headline: Kadazan-Dusun Language Speakers Decline 28% in One Generation
context: A 2025 Sabah cultural heritage survey found Kadazan-Dusun language fluency among those aged 15-30 is 28% lower than among those aged 45-60, indicating rapid intergenerational language loss.

cards.big: Kadazan-Dusun is Sabah's largest indigenous language.
cards.sub: Fluency among youth is 28% lower than their parents' generation. Without intervention, functional extinction is projected within 50 years.

cards.big: Kadazan-Dusun is taught in 124 schools under the POL programme.
cards.sub: But allocation is 3 hours per week. Malay receives 8 hours. English receives 6 hours.
Malaysia has 137 indigenous languages. 71 are classified as endangered or worse.

cards.big: Wales reversed Welsh language decline through mandatory bilingual education.
cards.sub: Youth fluency rose from 18% to 33% in 20 years. Institutional commitment changed the trajectory.

cards.big: 71 endangered languages is not a cultural detail. It is a mass extinction of identity.

Language death is reversible but only with institutional support — funding, education hours, media presence. Malaysia's indigenous languages need a national preservation strategy before the speakers are gone.
